title: Spatiotemporal Structures of Parametrically Driven Nonlinear Lattices
abstract: We theoretically investigate the effects of parametric driving on the one-dimensional Frenkel-Kontorova model, a nonlinear many-body lattice system. It is numerically found that a parametric vibration induces spatiotemporal ordering characterized by the subharmonic frequency $ω_{\mathrm{ex}}/2$ and wavenumber $k$ which takes nontrivial values depending on the vibration strength $P_{\mathrm{ac}}$ and frequency $ω_{\mathrm{ex}}$. With increasing $P_{\mathrm{ac}}$, $k$ gradually increases and discontinuously jumps up to $k=π$. Based on an extended linear stability analysis, we show that the former $k$ resonance (the latter $π$ resonance) corresponds to the unstable (stable) mode and that the $k$ resonance is made possible by the interplay between the parametric amplification and the collective fluctuation developing through the nonlinear effect.
summary: This paper explores how parametric driving creates complex spatiotemporal patterns in a nonlinear 1D lattice. It identifies a transition between different resonant states and explains how nonlinear effects and parametric amplification work together to select specific spatial wavenumbers.
main_problem: Investigating the formation mechanisms and stability of spatiotemporal structures, specifically nontrivial k-resonance and pi-resonance states, in parametrically driven nonlinear many-body lattices.
main_result: The study finds that parametric driving induces spatiotemporal ordering with a subharmonic frequency, where the wavenumber k increases with driving strength before jumping discontinuously to pi-resonance. The k-resonance is driven by the interplay between parametric amplification and nonlinear collective fluctuations.
method: Numerical simulations using the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method combined with analytical extended linear stability analysis and the method of multiple scales.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional Frenkel-Kontorova (FK) model consisting of elastically-coupled classical oscillators with an on-site potential and periodic parametric driving.
key_observables: Fourier spectrum S(k, omega), spatiotemporal patterns of phase, wavenumber k, and oscillation amplitude.
important_parameters: Parametric driving strength (Pac), driving frequency (omega_ex), coupling constant (K), damping (gamma), and noise strength (T).
main_assumptions: Small parameter epsilon for the method of multiple scales; single-mode approximation for the evolution of Fourier components; nonlinear approximation of the sine potential.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the schematic of pi-resonance and a phase diagram of wavenumber k; Fig 2 displays real-space spatiotemporal structures and Fourier spectra for different driving regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the FK model and parametric driving, presents numerical results on wavenumber selection and phase transitions, provides an analytical derivation of the resonance mechanism via stability analysis, and concludes with an investigation of domain wall dynamics in the supplemental material.
why_interesting: The study of pattern formation and stability in driven nonlinear lattices is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions and collective dynamics in many-body systems, which are central themes in both classical and quantum many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the π resonance appearing in the one-dimensional FK model with the parametric driving, cou- pled oscillators under vertical vibration with strength Pac and frequency ωex, where ϕn is the phase of the nth oscillator, and red (blue) regions in the space-time plane indicates positive (negative) values of ϕn. (b) Pac and ωex dependence of a spa- tiotemporal (ST) structure with wavenumber k. The color represents k/2π in the resonant state, while the white region denotes the off-resonant state. The result is obtained for the rest initial condition.

FIG. 2. ST structures (left panels) and the corresponding Fourier spectra (right panels) for ωex = 2.02 : (a) Pac = 0.007, (b) Pac = 0.024 , and (c) Pac = 0.05. In the left panels, horizontal and vertical axes denote the site index n and time t/tex , respectively, and the color represents the value of ϕn/π. In the right panels, the color represents the spectrum intensity S(k, ω), and the insets show zoomed views near the Fourier peaks at 2ω/ωex = 1.

FIG. 3. Phase diagrams obtained for the initial states of (a) the π configuration and (b) the mixed-phase configuration, where color notations are the same as those in Fig. 1(b). In (b), a dashed ellipse indicates the region where the mixed- phase method does not work. (c) Pac dependence of the wavenumber k, where red, green, and blue data correspond to the numerical results shown in Figs. 1(b), 3(a), and 3(b), respectively. The black solid line represents the analytical re- sult obtained selfconsistently from Eqs. (5) and (9).

Solitons.note: The new paper directly studies the Frenkel-Kontorova model and the formation of complex spatiotemporal structures and resonance states in nonlinear lattices.
